text:
RESTAGING CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND WHOLE-BODY BONE SCAN

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Marlena Okonkwo is a 39-year-old woman with metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma (primary diagnosis: left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, ER/PR/HER2-negative, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed). She has known metastatic disease involving the osseous skeleton (right 5th posterior rib, T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing) and liver (right lobe). She recently completed a short course of palliative external beam radiation to the right 5th rib and initiated olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg twice daily approximately 3 months prior. Zoledronic acid monthly also ongoing. This examination is performed for restaging and response assessment.

COMPARISON:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast dated approximately 3 months prior (prior study, obtained at time of initial distant recurrence workup). Whole-body bone scan from same prior date available for comparison.

---

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following the administration of 100 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast (portal venous phase acquisition for abdomen and pelvis; arterial and portal venous phases for hepatic evaluation). Scout images obtained. Axial 5 mm reconstructions with 1.25 mm reformats in coronal and sagittal planes reviewed.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: The lung parenchyma is clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation identified. No ground-glass opacities. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is midline. No pathologic mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The thymus is not enlarged. Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. Pericardium appears unremarkable.

Chest wall / Right 5th rib: The right 5th posterior rib demonstrates expected post-radiation changes with mild sclerotic reaction along the previously identified lytic lesion. The degree of cortical disruption is less conspicuous compared to the prior study. No new fracture. No adjacent soft tissue mass. Minimal residual tracer uptake expected on nuclear medicine correlation.

Left breast: Post-lumpectomy changes in the left upper outer quadrant with surgical clip artifact in the expected tumor bed. No abnormal skin thickening or new mass. Axillary soft tissues appear stable without lymphadenopathy.

Abdomen:
Liver: Two hypoattenuating lesions are again identified within the right hepatic lobe:
  - Segment VI/VII lesion: previously measured 1.2 cm, now measures approximately 0.9 cm in greatest dimension. The lesion appears less conspicuous on this examination with slightly increased attenuation on portal venous phase, suggesting some degree of treatment effect. Margins remain mildly indistinct.
  - Segment V lesion: previously measured 0.8 cm, now measures approximately 0.6 cm. Appearance is otherwise unchanged in character.
  No new hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent. No hepatic arterial irregularity.

Gallbladder: Not distended. No gallstones. No pericholecystic fluid.

Pancreas: Pancreatic parenchyma appears homogeneous without focal lesion, ductal dilation, or peripancreatic fat stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Adrenal glands: No adrenal masses bilaterally. Right and left adrenal glands appear normal.

Kidneys: Normal bilateral renal enhancement and excretion. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. Small bilateral cortical cysts, benign in appearance.

Gastrointestinal: Small bowel and large bowel are not obstructed. No mesenteric or omental lesions. No free intraperitoneal fluid.

Aorta and vessels: Abdominal aorta is normal caliber. No aneurysm. No vascular compression.

Pelvis:
Right iliac wing: Previously identified lytic lesion in the right iliac wing measuring 1.8 cm again identified. Morphologic appearance is stable on this examination without appreciable change in size or degree of cortical disruption. No new adjacent soft tissue component.

Left iliac wing and sacrum: Unremarkable. No new lytic or sclerotic lesions.

Uterus and adnexa: Uterus is anteverted and within normal limits for age. No adnexal masses. No pelvic free fluid.

Lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged pelvic, retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or inguinal lymph nodes.

Soft tissues: No subcutaneous or deep soft tissue masses. No edema.

Bone windows (CT):
In addition to the lesions described above, osseous structures otherwise appear unremarkable. No new lytic or blastic lesions identified on review of bone windows throughout the spine, pelvis, and visualized proximal long bones. The T10 vertebral body is better evaluated on the nuclear medicine study; no new CT-visible lesion is identified at this level on this examination.

---

WHOLE-BODY BONE SCAN (TECHNETIUM-99m MDP SCINTIGRAPHY)

TECHNIQUE:
Static planar whole-body bone scintigraphy performed at 3-hour post-injection interval following administration of 25 mCi of Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate. Anterior and posterior views obtained. SPECT/CT of the thoracic spine and pelvis performed for correlative localization.

FINDINGS:

Right 5th rib (posterior): Residual mildly increased radiotracer uptake identified at the right 5th posterior rib at the site of prior palliative radiation. Uptake is less intense compared to the prior scan, compatible with radiation effect and treatment response. No adjacent new foci.

T10 vertebral body: Focal increased uptake in the T10 vertebral body is again noted, stable in intensity and distribution compared to the prior examination. No extension to adjacent levels. SPECT/CT correlation confirms this corresponds to the known osseous lesion without paraspinal soft tissue extension.

Right iliac wing: Increased radiotracer activity in the right iliac wing is again identified, stable compared to prior bone scan. SPECT/CT localizes this to the previously characterized lytic lesion. No new foci of increased activity in the pelvis.

Remainder of skeleton: No new abnormal foci of radiotracer uptake identified throughout the calvarium, facial bones, cervical spine, thoracic spine above T10, lumbar spine below T10, ribs (excluding right 5th), sternum, clavicles, shoulders, bilateral upper extremities, or bilateral lower extremities. Normal distribution of radiotracer in the kidneys and bladder. Mild degenerative uptake at the bilateral knees and left shoulder, consistent with known age-related changes.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. The two right lobe hepatic lesions have decreased in size compared to the prior study -- the larger lesion now measures 0.9 cm (previously 1.2 cm) and the smaller lesion now measures 0.6 cm (previously 0.8 cm). This is consistent with a meaningful response to olaparib therapy, with no new hepatic lesions.

2. The known osseous lesions at the right 5th posterior rib, T10 vertebral body, and right iliac wing are stable in appearance on both CT and bone scan. The right 5th rib shows expected post-radiation sclerotic changes with diminished uptake on bone scan, consistent with local treatment effect following palliative radiotherapy. No evidence of fracture at any site.

3. No new sites of osseous, hepatic, pulmonary, lymph nodal, or visceral disease identified.

4. Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a favorable response to ongoing systemic therapy, with hepatic disease clearly smaller and known bone sites stable.
